heroOverview: Volvulus is the abnormal axial rotation of a segment of bowel around its mesenteric axis, creating a closed-loop obstruction that simultaneously blocks the passage of intestinal contents and compresses the mesenteric blood vessels. Without prompt treatment, full-thickness bowel necrosis begins within hours, making it one of the true surgical emergencies of the abdomen. Sigmoid volvulus — torsion of the sigmoid colon — accounts for approximately 60-75% of all adult cases and is the most common cause of large-bowel obstruction in Africa, Asia, and parts of South America, where it drives up to 50% of intestinal obstruction cases in some series. Caecal volvulus, the second most common form, is more prevalent in younger women. Midgut volvulus in neonates and young infants represents a separate, catastrophic entity arising from malrotation of the gut during fetal development. The characteristic plain-abdominal-radiograph appearance — a massively dilated loop curving up from the pelvis ('coffee-bean' or 'omega' sign for sigmoid; 'whirl' sign for caecal) — is classic, and rapid endoscopic detorsion is successful in 70-90% of uncomplicated sigmoid cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Sigmoid volvulus causes approximately 3-5% of bowel obstruction in developed countries and up to 50% in parts of Africa, Asia, and Brazil; caecal volvulus accounts for 11% of all colonic volvuli in the USA
keyStats.demographics: Sigmoid volvulus peaks in elderly institutionalised men (mean age 65-75); caecal volvulus more common in women aged 30-60; midgut volvulus presents in infants under one year
keyStats.avgAge: Sigmoid volvulus: median age approximately 70 years in high-income countries; caecal volvulus: median age approximately 50 years; midgut volvulus: 80% of cases in children under one year
keyStats.globalCases: Globally sigmoid volvulus is a major cause of bowel obstruction, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa (Ethiopia, Uganda) and the 'volvulus belt' from West Africa to South Asia, where up to 30% of all intestinal obstructions are sigmoid volvulus

definition: Volvulus (ICD-10: K56.2) is an axial rotation of a bowel segment around the longitudinal axis of its mesentery, producing a closed-loop obstruction. A closed-loop obstruction is particularly dangerous because the loop is blocked at both ends simultaneously, preventing decompression; intraluminal pressure rises rapidly, impairing mucosal blood flow first, then causing progressive full-thickness ischaemia and, ultimately, perforation if reduction is not achieved. The bowel segments most susceptible to volvulus are those with a long, relatively mobile mesentery tethered at a narrow base — the sigmoid colon and caecum in adults, and the entire midgut in fetuses whose intestinal rotation is incomplete. In sigmoid volvulus, a redundant, elongated sigmoid loop twists on its mesenteric pedicle; predisposing factors include a high-fibre diet producing a chronically distended colon, long-term constipation, Chagas disease (a parasitic infection that destroys myenteric ganglia), and immobility. Caecal volvulus occurs when the ascending colon and terminal ileum share an abnormally mobile mesentery (caecal non-fixation), allowing a fold or rotation of the caecum anteriorly and superiorly. Gastric volvulus, twisting of the stomach, is rare but always requires urgent intervention. Midgut volvulus in neonates is associated with malrotation and presents with bilious vomiting — bile-stained green vomit in any newborn is a surgical emergency until malrotation with volvulus is excluded. The condition falls under emergency surgery and gastroenterology.

typesClassification.description: The most common type globally; a long redundant sigmoid colon twists on its mesentery. Accounts for 60-75% of cases. Presents with massive abdominal distension, absolute constipation, and crampy lower abdominal pain. The 'coffee-bean' or 'bent inner tube' sign on plain X-ray is characteristic.

The mobile caecum and terminal ileum rotate together; there are two subtypes — axial rotation and caecal bascule (folding). Presents with right-sided pain and a palpable mass; less amenable to endoscopic detorsion than sigmoid volvulus and more often requires surgery.

The entire small bowel twists en masse due to congenital malrotation; the superior mesenteric artery is compressed, threatening the entire midgut with necrosis. Presents with bilious (green) vomiting, often in the first days of life. This is an absolute surgical emergency.

Twisting of the stomach, usually around the organoaxial (longitudinal) axis; often associated with a large hiatus or diaphragmatic hernia. Classically presents with Borchardt's triad: sudden severe upper abdominal pain, non-productive retching, and inability to pass a nasogastric tube.

Rare, accounting for fewer than 4% of all colonic volvuli; often associated with Hirschsprung disease, chronic constipation, or a previous sigmoid volvulus operation. Presents similarly to sigmoid volvulus with massive colonic distension.

primarySymptoms: Sudden severe abdominal pain, often cramping, that may be constant or in waves, typically beginning in the lower abdomen for sigmoid volvulus or across the mid-abdomen for caecal and midgut forms.
Massive abdominal distension — the hallmark of sigmoid volvulus — producing a visible, sometimes asymmetric bloating of the entire abdomen that can be extreme, with the loop tympanic to percussion.
Absolute constipation with complete failure to pass stool or flatus, distinguishing complete volvulus from simple constipation where gas may still be passed.
Nausea and vomiting, which tends to develop later and become bilious (green or yellow-green) as proximal small-bowel obstruction supervenes — bilious vomiting in a newborn is always an emergency.
Tender, tympanic abdomen on examination; in early uncomplicated cases the abdomen is soft between spasms, but peritoneal signs develop rapidly with ischaemia.
Rapid deterioration to systemic signs of bowel ischaemia: tachycardia, fever, hypotension, and a rigid, silent abdomen with rebound tenderness — indicating perforation and peritonitis.
Borchardt's triad in gastric volvulus: epigastric pain, unproductive retching, and inability to pass a nasogastric tube into the stomach.
Paradoxical loose stools occasionally in the early phase of sigmoid volvulus, as material distal to the twist is evacuated while new stool accumulates proximally.
Visible peristaltic waves on the abdominal wall in thin patients with a distended obstructed small bowel proximal to a caecal or midgut volvulus.
In neonates: bilious vomiting from day one to three of life, abdominal distension, and rapid cardiovascular collapse if the superior mesenteric artery is completely occluded.
earlyWarningSigns: A history of prior episodes of abdominal distension that resolved spontaneously, consistent with a partially twisting sigmoid colon that untwisted on its own — a pattern known as recurrent sigmoid volvulus.
Progressive inability to pass flatus over six to twelve hours in an elderly patient with known chronic constipation.
A unilateral or asymmetric abdominal bulge visible from the bedside in a frail patient.
Bilious (green) vomiting in any newborn — this requires emergency evaluation until midgut volvulus is excluded.
emergencySigns: Abdominal rigidity, guarding, and rebound tenderness indicating peritonitis from perforation or gangrenous bowel.
Systemic sepsis — fever above 38.5°C, heart rate above 120, low blood pressure, and confusion — signalling full-thickness bowel necrosis with bacterial translocation.
Bilious vomiting in a newborn or young infant, which is a surgical emergency requiring imaging within hours.
Inability to decompress an acutely distended abdomen with a nasogastric tube, suggesting gastric volvulus with complete outflow obstruction.

causes.cause: Anatomically redundant or elongated sigmoid colon with narrow mesenteric base
causes.description: A sigma with an unusually long free loop anchored on a relatively narrow mesenteric pedicle creates the mechanical precondition for torsion; the long lever arm of the loop allows it to rotate with peristaltic forces, and once 180° of rotation occurs the loop cannot easily untwist spontaneously. This anatomical variant is genetically and constitutionally determined and is more prevalent in certain populations.

causes.cause: Chagas disease and other causes of megacolon
causes.description: Trypanosoma cruzi infection destroys the myenteric ganglia of the sigmoid colon, producing a chronically dilated, atonic sigmoid megacolon that is dramatically susceptible to torsion. Chagas disease-related volvulus is the dominant etiology in parts of South America and accounts for a significant proportion of cases in endemic regions.

causes.cause: Chronic severe constipation and faecal loading
causes.description: Prolonged constipation in institutionalised or bedbound patients produces a chronically loaded, heavy, and distended sigmoid colon that is mechanically more likely to rotate under the influence of peristaltic waves. High-fibre staple diets in regions with high sigmoid volvulus rates contribute via chronic colonic distension.

During fetal development the midgut rotates 270° anticlockwise to reach its final configuration, anchoring the small bowel from the ligament of Treitz to the right iliac fossa. Failure of this rotation leaves the bowel on a narrow, mobile mesentery vulnerable to volvulus around the superior mesenteric artery axis. Ladd's bands, formed during abnormal rotation, can additionally obstruct the duodenum.

In approximately 11% of the population, the right colon fails to fully adhere to the posterior peritoneum during fetal development, leaving the caecum and ascending colon mobile. Under mechanical stress — a sudden increase in intra-abdominal pressure, position change, or constipation — the caecum rotates superiorly and anteriorly, causing caecal volvulus.

Adhesive bands from previous surgery can act as a fixed pivot point around which a bowel loop rotates, while simultaneously restricting its ability to untwist spontaneously. Post-operative adhesion-related volvulus is a distinct mechanism from the free-rotation type and may require adhesiolysis in addition to detorsion at the time of surgical treatment.

riskFactors.description: Sigmoid volvulus is 5-10 times more common in adults over 65, particularly those in nursing homes, psychiatric institutions, or with dementia; immobility, opioid use, and baseline constipation compound anatomical predisposition.
Men are affected by sigmoid volvulus 3-4 times more often than women; the male pelvis offers less space to accommodate a redundant sigmoid, increasing the mechanical tendency to twist.

riskFactors.factor: High-residue diet and chronic colonic distension
riskFactors.description: Populations with very high dietary fibre intake and limited access to healthcare show sigmoid volvulus rates up to 20 times those seen in low-income Western countries, attributed to chronically overloaded, elongated sigmoid colons.

T. cruzi-induced colonic dysmotility and megacolon dramatically increase sigmoid volvulus risk in endemic regions of South and Central America; the risk accumulates over the years of chronic infection.

Long-standing constipation, particularly in elderly, demented, or opioid-treated patients, produces a loaded colon that is mechanically susceptible; addressing constipation with appropriate bowel regimens is a practical prevention strategy.

Present in approximately 0.2-1% of the general population; most cases are asymptomatic for years but all carry lifelong risk of midgut volvulus. First-degree relatives of affected individuals may have incomplete rotation.

Found in approximately 11% of adults on imaging; the risk of caecal volvulus over a lifetime is low in absolute terms but is higher in women of childbearing age, possibly due to pelvic floor changes from pregnancy.

Without definitive surgical treatment (sigmoid resection or caecal fixation), recurrence rates after endoscopic detorsion alone are 40-90%, making elective surgery an important risk-reduction step after an index episode.

affectedDemographics: Elderly institutionalised men aged 65-80 for sigmoid volvulus
Neonates and infants under one year for midgut volvulus associated with malrotation
People with Chagas disease in endemic regions of South and Central America
Patients with chronic constipation, neurological disease, or long-term opioid use
Those with prior abdominal surgery and adhesions
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of volvulus begins with the clinical presentation and plain abdominal radiographs, which show characteristic signs in a substantial proportion of cases. The sigmoid volvulus coffee-bean or omega sign — a massively dilated sigmoid loop with its apex pointing to the right upper quadrant and the two limbs of the loop converging to the left pelvis — is present and diagnostic in approximately 60-80% of cases on a plain supine abdominal film. The caecal volvulus produces a dilated air-filled caecum displaced to the left upper or mid abdomen with a characteristic 'whirl' pattern of mesenteric fat on CT. Water-soluble contrast enema (barium is avoided if perforation is possible) confirms the diagnosis and in the same session can be used therapeutically in sigmoid volvulus, showing abrupt cut-off of contrast at the twist site with a 'bird's beak' or 'ace of spades' tapering. CT of the abdomen with IV contrast is the definitive imaging modality in uncertain or complex cases, offering the highest sensitivity and specificity; it identifies the whirl sign of twisted mesenteric vessels and fat, detects pneumatosis (gas in the bowel wall indicating ischaemia), and assesses for perforation. CT is mandatory in adult cases of caecal volvulus and when ischaemia is suspected. Flexible sigmoidoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic in uncomplicated sigmoid volvulus — it confirms the diagnosis by directly visualising the twisted mucosa at the rectosigmoid junction, assesses mucosal viability, and allows decompression via passage of a flatus tube through the twist. Upper gastrointestinal contrast study and ultrasound are the primary investigations for suspected neonatal midgut volvulus, with CT avoided in infants when possible due to radiation concern.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line rapid investigation: identifies the massively dilated sigmoid loop (coffee-bean sign), displaced caecum, or small-bowel obstruction pattern, and shows free air if perforation has occurred.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two brief X-rays of the abdomen taken within minutes; no preparation or contrast required. Results visible immediately. Diagnostic in 60-80% of sigmoid volvulus cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis in complex, uncertain, or adult cases; identifies the whirl sign of mesenteric torsion, assesses bowel viability (pneumatosis, free fluid), and characterises any underlying cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute scan; an IV cannula is placed for contrast injection. You lie still inside the scanner ring. Results available within an hour. Radiation dose equivalent to about 3 years of background radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Simultaneously confirms sigmoid volvulus, assesses the rectal and sigmoid mucosa for ischaemia or necrosis, and decompresses the distended loop by advancing a flatus tube through the twist.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible camera is passed through the rectum to the level of the twist; brief discomfort is expected. Performed without sedation in most emergency settings. A long flatus tube is left in place for 24-48 hours to prevent immediate re-twist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the bird's-beak narrowing at the twist site and can be used therapeutically to attempt hydrostatic reduction of sigmoid volvulus in selected cases without endoscopic facilities.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast fluid is introduced gently through a tube in the rectum while images are taken with fluoroscopy. Performed lying on the X-ray table; mild cramping is common.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the characteristic corkscrew pattern of the malrotated duodenum and proximal jejunum in neonatal midgut volvulus, confirming the diagnosis before emergency Ladd's procedure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The neonate swallows a small amount of water-soluble contrast; continuous fluoroscopic images follow its passage through the duodenum and proximal jejunum. Takes 15-30 minutes; performed with specialist radiologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the 'whirlpool' sign of superior mesenteric vein rotating around the superior mesenteric artery on Doppler imaging, confirming midgut volvulus with high sensitivity in experienced centres.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless gel-and-probe examination of the neonate's abdomen taking 5-10 minutes; no radiation; performed at the bedside if the infant is too unstable to transport.

treatmentOverview: The urgency and choice of treatment depend on the site of volvulus, whether the bowel is viable, and the patient's overall condition. For sigmoid volvulus without peritonitis or suspected necrosis, flexible sigmoidoscopy with flatus tube decompression is the first-line treatment: it is successful in approximately 70-90% of cases and allows the patient to be stabilised and the colon prepared for definitive elective sigmoid resection. Water-soluble contrast enema reduction is an alternative when sigmoidoscopy is unavailable. After successful endoscopic decompression, elective sigmoid resection is strongly recommended because endoscopic detorsion alone carries a recurrence rate of 40-90% within weeks to months; operative mortality for elective resection (around 1-5%) is far lower than for emergency resection after recurrence or perforation (15-25%). When the bowel is suspected or confirmed to be necrotic, or when sigmoidoscopy fails, emergency surgery is mandatory; Hartmann's procedure — sigmoid resection with end colostomy and closure of the rectal stump — is the most common emergency operation when peritoneal contamination is present. Caecal volvulus is not amenable to endoscopic decompression in most cases and typically requires emergency right hemicolectomy; caecal fixation (caecopexy) without resection has higher recurrence rates than resection and is generally preferred only in high-risk surgical candidates. Midgut volvulus in neonates requires emergency laparotomy and Ladd's procedure within hours of diagnosis: the volvulus is untwisted, Ladd's bands are divided, the caecum is placed in the left lower quadrant, and any gangrenous bowel is resected. Total parenteral nutrition may be required long-term if extensive small-bowel resection produces short-bowel syndrome. Post-operatively, bowel rest, nasogastric decompression, and careful fluid management are standard; stoma management education is provided to all patients leaving hospital with a colostomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Endoscopic decompression (flexible sigmoidoscopy with flatus tube)
medicalTreatments.description: A flexible scope is advanced to the level of the twist, confirming the diagnosis and assessing mucosa; if mucosa appears viable (pink, with intact folds), a long soft flatus tube (50-60 cm) is advanced through the twist, allowing the trapped gas and faecal material to decompress. The tube is left in place for 24-48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful decompression achieved in approximately 70-90% of cases with viable sigmoid mucosa; recurrence without definitive surgery is 40-90% over the following months, necessitating elective resection.

medicalTreatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation and nasogastric decompression
medicalTreatments.description: All patients with volvulus require aggressive IV fluid replacement to correct dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, and haemodynamic instability; a nasogastric tube decompresses the stomach and prevents aspiration of vomit during induction of anaesthesia if surgery follows.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential for haemodynamic stability before any intervention; mortality is significantly higher when patients are operated on before adequate resuscitation.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: Antibiotics covering gram-negative and anaerobic bowel flora are administered before surgery and continued for 24-48 hours post-operatively. They are also given before endoscopic decompression in patients with suspected mucosal ischaemia or systemic inflammatory signs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Perioperative antibiotics reduce post-operative intra-abdominal infection and wound sepsis by approximately 50% per established colorectal surgery quality standards.

surgicalOptions.name: Elective sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis
surgicalOptions.description: Performed four to six weeks after successful endoscopic decompression with bowel preparation; the redundant sigmoid colon is removed laparoscopically or open, and the bowel ends are joined immediately. This provides definitive cure with a recurrence rate below 5%.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive cure in approximately 95% of cases; operative mortality in elective setting 1-3%; anastomotic leak in approximately 2-5%.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency Hartmann's procedure
surgicalOptions.description: The sigmoid colon is resected and the proximal end brought out as an end colostomy (Hartmann's colostomy); the rectal stump is closed and left in place. Performed when peritoneal contamination, haemodynamic instability, or unprepared bowel makes primary anastomosis unsafe. Colostomy reversal is planned at six months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence of volvulus after resection is below 2%; colostomy reversal is achieved in approximately 50-75% of patients; overall operative mortality in emergency setting is 15-25% reflecting the severity of the presentation.

surgicalOptions.name: Right hemicolectomy (caecal volvulus)
surgicalOptions.description: Resection of the caecum, ascending colon, and terminal ileum with ileocolic anastomosis; the standard treatment for caecal volvulus. Provides definitive treatment and avoids the high recurrence rate of caecopexy alone.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Near 100% definitive cure for caecal volvulus; anastomotic leak rate approximately 2-5%; emergency mortality depends on bowel viability at presentation.

surgicalOptions.name: Ladd's procedure (midgut volvulus)
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency surgical untwisting of the midgut volvulus, division of Ladd's bands obstructing the duodenum, appendicectomy, and repositioning of the bowel (caecum in left lower quadrant, small bowel on the right) to widen the mesenteric base and reduce future rotation risk. Necrotic bowel is resected.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Survival approaches 100% when performed before bowel necrosis; when extensive bowel necrosis is present, mortality and the risk of short-bowel syndrome are substantial. Recurrence after Ladd's procedure is approximately 5-10%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Conservative bowel management for recurrent partial sigmoid volvulus
alternativeTreatments.description: In patients who are frail, at extreme surgical risk, and whose sigmoid volvulus partially detorts with enemas, laxatives, and postural manoeuvres, a programme of regular colonoscopic deflation combined with stool softeners and mobility assistance can reduce the frequency of acute episodes. This is a palliative strategy, not a cure.

alternativeTreatments.name: Percutaneous endoscopic colostomy (PEC)
alternativeTreatments.description: A technique where endoscopic fixation of the sigmoid colon to the anterior abdominal wall is combined with a percutaneous tube for ongoing decompression; reported in case series for patients unfit for surgery. Evidence is limited to small series and it is considered a last resort.

whySeeSpecialist: Any suspected volvulus is a gastroenterological and surgical emergency requiring immediate hospital assessment. A gastroenterologist or colorectal surgeon with endoscopic expertise should perform the initial sigmoidoscopic decompression in sigmoid volvulus, with surgical backup immediately available. In neonatal midgut volvulus, a paediatric surgeon must be consulted urgently. Elective follow-up resection should be performed by a colorectal surgeon with laparoscopic expertise to minimise operative risk and ensure long-term cure.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute volvulus, the priority is the nearest emergency department with access to general surgery and endoscopy; this is not a condition where time should be spent selecting a preferred provider. For elective follow-up resection after sigmoid decompression, a colorectal surgeon with laparoscopic sigmoid resection experience and a low anastomotic leak rate provides the best outcomes. Centres with high-volume colorectal surgery have demonstrably lower morbidity for these procedures.
preventionStrategies: After successful endoscopic decompression of sigmoid volvulus, do not delay elective resection — the 40-90% recurrence rate without surgery means a second emergency, with higher mortality, is very likely.

Aggressively treat chronic constipation in elderly, institutionalised, or opioid-treated patients with osmotic laxatives, adequate hydration, and mobility; preventing severe faecal loading reduces the mechanical risk of torsion.
Diagnose and treat Chagas disease in endemic regions; while established megacolon is not reversible, managing active infection limits progression.

When intestinal malrotation is diagnosed incidentally in children or asymptomatic adults, a Ladd's procedure as an elective operation prevents the catastrophic midgut volvulus that can occur at any time.
Educate parents and caregivers that bilious (green) vomiting in a newborn requires immediate emergency evaluation to exclude midgut volvulus.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain regular bowel habits with adequate fluid intake, dietary fibre in moderation, and daily physical activity to prevent the constipation that predisposes to sigmoid volvulus.
Avoid prolonged immobility; daily walking, even in frail elderly patients, significantly reduces constipation risk and supports colonic motility.
For patients who have had a colostomy after Hartmann's procedure, attend stoma care nurse appointments and ensure stoma supplies are accessible before discharge.
After elective sigmoid resection, a low-residue diet for four to six weeks allows the anastomosis to heal; fibre can be gradually reintroduced thereafter.
Report any return of abdominal distension, pain, or inability to pass gas after treatment of a volvulus, as recurrence requires immediate re-evaluation.

recommended: Regular meals with consistent, moderate dietary fibre to support regular bowel habit without producing chronic colonic overloading
Adequate daily fluid intake (1.5-2 litres) to maintain soft stool consistency and prevent the faecal impaction that loads the sigmoid
Post-surgical low-residue diet (white rice, cooked vegetables, lean protein) for four to six weeks while bowel continuity heals
Probiotic foods to support colonic microbiome and reduce bacterial translocation risk after bowel surgery
avoid: Very high-fibre diets producing chronic colonic distension in patients known to have a redundant sigmoid colon
Opioid pain medications except when essential, as they dramatically worsen constipation and sigmoid loading; if opioids are required, add a stimulant laxative
High-gas-producing foods such as beans, cruciferous vegetables, and carbonated drinks in the acute post-operative period
Dehydration and low fluid intake, which harden stool and promote the faecal loading that increases volvulus risk

exerciseGuidelines: Daily walking and regular physical activity are the most effective lifestyle measures to maintain colonic motility and prevent the constipation that predisposes to sigmoid volvulus; they are strongly encouraged in all patients who are medically stable. After emergency surgery with bowel resection, return to light activity in two to four weeks and full activity in six to eight weeks is typical; heavy lifting is avoided for six to eight weeks while the abdominal wall heals. For patients with a stoma, exercise is fully permitted but contact sports should avoid direct blows to the stoma site.
dailyManagement: After endoscopic decompression and while awaiting elective resection, follow a regular bowel regimen with osmotic laxatives (such as macrogol/polyethylene glycol) to prevent faecal reloading of the sigmoid.

Report any recurrence of abdominal distension, inability to pass flatus, or colicky lower abdominal pain immediately — do not wait, as recurrent volvulus can perforate more rapidly than the index episode.

Stoma patients should have daily stoma bag checks, know the normal output colour and volume, and contact a stoma nurse if output drops suddenly or if the stoma changes colour (a discoloured or dusky stoma may indicate ischaemia).

Newborns who have undergone Ladd's procedure require close nutritional monitoring and developmental follow-up; short-bowel syndrome, if extensive resection was needed, requires specialist nutritional support long-term.

Follow up with the colorectal surgeon within four to six weeks of discharge after both emergency and elective operations to review pathology results, wound healing, and to plan stoma reversal if applicable.
prognosis: Outcomes for volvulus depend critically on whether bowel viability is intact at the time of treatment. For sigmoid volvulus treated by successful endoscopic decompression followed by elective resection, mortality is below 3% and long-term cure approaches 95%. Emergency surgery for non-viable or perforated sigmoid bowel carries a mortality of 15-25% in older series and 10-20% in contemporary high-income centres, reflecting the age and frailty of the typical patient. Caecal volvulus requires surgery in most cases; mortality in elective right hemicolectomy is 1-3%, rising to 10-15% in emergency cases with ischaemia. Neonatal midgut volvulus treated by prompt Ladd's procedure before bowel necrosis has a survival rate approaching 100% in experienced paediatric surgical centres; when extensive small-bowel necrosis is present, long-term short-bowel syndrome requiring parenteral nutrition is a major life-affecting outcome. Sigmoid volvulus has a high recurrence rate of 40-90% after endoscopic decompression alone, which is why elective resection after a first episode is widely recommended to prevent a second, potentially fatal emergency presentation.
recoveryTimeline: After successful endoscopic decompression of sigmoid volvulus, patients typically have the flatus tube removed after 24-48 hours and begin a low-residue liquid diet; hospital discharge within two to four days is usual if no complications develop. Elective sigmoid resection is scheduled four to six weeks later; laparoscopic resection allows discharge in three to four days, with full recovery in two to four weeks. After emergency Hartmann's procedure, hospital stay is seven to fourteen days; stoma reversal, if planned, is typically performed six months later with a separate surgical admission. Neonates after Ladd's procedure are in hospital for one to three weeks; return of bowel function and tolerating full feeds is the discharge criterion.
complications: Bowel necrosis and perforation — occurs when ischaemia from the closed-loop obstruction progresses to full-thickness wall death; carries a mortality of 15-25% in adults and requires emergency resection.
Peritonitis — faecal contamination of the peritoneum from perforation requires emergency surgery and prolonged antibiotic treatment, with high rates of post-operative intra-abdominal abscess.
Anastomotic leak — a dehiscence of the bowel join after resection occurring in approximately 2-5% of cases, requiring reoperation or CT-guided drainage.

Short-bowel syndrome — following extensive small-bowel resection for midgut volvulus with necrosis, the remaining bowel may be insufficient to absorb adequate nutrition, requiring long-term parenteral nutrition.
Recurrence after endoscopic decompression alone — affects 40-90% of patients who do not undergo elective resection, with each subsequent episode carrying higher mortality.
Stoma-related complications including stoma prolapse, retraction, skin breakdown, and parastomal hernia in approximately 20-30% of patients who undergo Hartmann's procedure.

supportResources.name: NHS — Bowel obstruction
supportResources.description: UK patient information on bowel obstruction including volvulus, covering symptoms, emergency treatment, and recovery.

supportResources.name: United Ostomy Associations of America
supportResources.description: Support, education, and community resources for patients living with a stoma after colorectal surgery.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Bowel obstruction and volvulus
supportResources.description: U.S. National Institutes overview of bowel obstruction types including volvulus, with information for patients and caregivers.

sources.title: Perrot L et al. Colonic Volvulus: The Best Elective Treatment Is Resection. Journal of Visceral Surgery, 2016
Ballantyne GH. Review of Sigmoid Volvulus: History and Results of Treatment. Diseases of the Colon and Rectum, 1982
Halabi WJ et al. Colonic Volvulus in the United States: Trends, Outcomes, and Predictors of Mortality. Annals of Surgery, 2014
Durkin ET et al. Intestinal Malrotation and Volvulus: What Every Clinician Should Know. Journal of Pediatric Surgery, 2006

confusedWithConditions.name: Intussusception
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Intussusception telescopes one segment into another and is the more common cause of obstruction in infants aged 5-9 months; volvulus twists the bowel and is more common in elderly adults (sigmoid) or neonates with malrotation (midgut). Imaging clearly distinguishes them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Paralytic ileus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Paralytic ileus produces uniform colonic and small-bowel dilation from absent peristalsis without a single obstructing twist; it lacks the whirl sign and massive single-loop distension of volvulus, and plain X-rays usually show diffuse gas throughout the colon.

confusedWithConditions.name: Intestinal adhesions with complete obstruction
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Adhesion obstruction typically follows prior surgery and produces small-bowel obstruction at a transition point between distended and collapsed loops; it lacks the characteristic rotational whirl sign and massive colonic loop of volvulus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Generalised acute peritonitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Late-stage perforated volvulus produces peritonitis that is clinically indistinguishable from other causes; CT distinguishes the originating volvulus with free perforation from primary peritonitis of other causes such as perforated peptic ulcer or appendix.

relatedConditions.name: Intussusception
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are mechanical bowel obstructions causing ischaemia; intussusception and volvulus together account for the majority of surgical bowel emergencies in children, and must be distinguished promptly as treatment approaches differ.

relatedConditions.name: Paralytic ileus
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-operative paralytic ileus may follow emergency volvulus surgery; recognising and managing ileus is part of the recovery pathway after bowel resection for volvulus.

relatedConditions.name: Intestinal adhesions with complete obstruction
relatedConditions.relevance: Adhesions are the most common cause of small-bowel obstruction in adults and an important differential diagnosis for volvulus; they can also create a fixed pivot point around which volvulus occurs.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Colorectal cancer can obstruct the sigmoid colon in a way that mimics sigmoid volvulus on plain films; CT distinguishes them, and both require urgent surgical evaluation.

faqs.question: What is volvulus?
faqs.answer: Volvulus is when a segment of the intestine twists around itself, blocking the passage of stool and gas and cutting off the blood supply to the twisted segment. Without treatment within hours, the bowel can be permanently damaged. It most commonly affects the sigmoid colon in older adults and the entire small bowel in newborns with a congenital gut-development problem.

faqs.question: What causes volvulus?
faqs.answer: Sigmoid volvulus is usually caused by a long, redundant sigmoid colon that twists around its narrow mesenteric stalk, often promoted by chronic constipation or immobility. Caecal volvulus results from a mobile caecum that was not anchored to the abdominal wall during development. In newborns, midgut volvulus is caused by incomplete rotation of the gut during fetal development.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of volvulus?
faqs.answer: Sudden severe abdominal pain, massive abdominal distension, and inability to pass stool or gas are the key symptoms. Nausea and vomiting follow as obstruction worsens. In a newborn, bilious (green) vomiting is the warning sign. As the bowel loses its blood supply, the abdomen becomes tender and rigid and the patient becomes seriously unwell.

faqs.question: Is volvulus a surgical emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. The closed-loop obstruction cuts off the blood supply to the trapped bowel, which can die within hours. Any suspected volvulus requires emergency hospital assessment. Sigmoid volvulus can sometimes be relieved non-operatively with a flexible camera, but most cases ultimately need surgery; midgut volvulus in newborns always requires emergency surgery.

faqs.question: How is sigmoid volvulus treated?
faqs.answer: The initial treatment is passing a flexible camera into the rectum (sigmoidoscopy) to decompress the trapped gas, which works in 70-90% of uncomplicated cases. A soft tube is left in place for 24-48 hours to prevent re-twisting. After recovery, elective surgical removal of the redundant sigmoid colon is strongly recommended to prevent the 40-90% recurrence risk.

faqs.question: What is caecal volvulus?
faqs.answer: Caecal volvulus is when the caecum (the beginning of the large bowel) and part of the small bowel twist together. It is more common in younger women and is caused by the caecum not being properly attached to the abdominal wall from birth. Unlike sigmoid volvulus, it usually cannot be untwisted with a scope and requires surgery.

faqs.question: What is midgut volvulus in a newborn?
faqs.answer: Midgut volvulus is when the entire small bowel twists around the main intestinal blood vessel in a newborn whose gut did not rotate correctly during fetal development. The blood supply to most of the intestine is at risk. Any newborn with bilious (green) vomiting needs emergency evaluation for this condition, as surgery within hours of diagnosis is required.

faqs.question: What is the coffee-bean sign in volvulus?
faqs.answer: The coffee-bean sign is the appearance of a massively dilated sigmoid colon loop on a plain abdominal X-ray; it looks like a large rounded double-chamber shape, resembling a coffee bean, pointing towards the right upper abdomen. This sign is present in about 60-80% of sigmoid volvulus cases and allows rapid diagnosis without CT scanning.

faqs.question: What happens if volvulus is not treated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, the blood supply to the twisted bowel is completely cut off, the bowel dies, and it perforates, releasing bacteria into the abdomen and causing life-threatening peritonitis and sepsis. Mortality from untreated or late-diagnosed volvulus is very high, particularly in elderly patients. Even with emergency surgery, mortality in perforated cases is 15-25%.

faqs.question: Will I need a colostomy after volvulus surgery?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. If the bowel is in good condition when the surgeon operates, the two cut ends can be joined immediately (primary anastomosis), avoiding a stoma. If the bowel is gangrenous or the abdomen is contaminated, a temporary colostomy (Hartmann's procedure) is safer; the colostomy is then reversed in a second operation about six months later, which is successful in approximately 50-75% of patients.

faqs.question: Can volvulus come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, and this is a major concern with endoscopic decompression alone: 40-90% of patients have a recurrence without definitive sigmoid resection. This is why elective surgery is strongly recommended after a first episode is successfully treated non-operatively. After surgical resection of the sigmoid colon, the recurrence rate drops to under 2%.

faqs.question: What is Ladd's procedure?
faqs.answer: Ladd's procedure is the emergency operation for midgut volvulus in newborns. The surgeon untwists the volvulus, cuts the fibrous bands (Ladd's bands) blocking the duodenum, removes any dead bowel, and repositions the intestine to widen the base of the mesentery, reducing the risk of re-twisting. Any necrotic bowel is resected at the same time.

faqs.question: How is volvulus diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A plain abdominal X-ray showing the characteristic massively dilated loop is the first test; it is diagnostic in 60-80% of sigmoid volvulus cases. CT scanning with IV contrast is the most accurate test, showing the whirl sign of twisted mesentery and assessing blood supply. In newborns, an upper GI contrast X-ray or abdominal ultrasound is used to confirm midgut volvulus.

faqs.question: What is the link between Chagas disease and volvulus?
faqs.answer: Chagas disease, caused by a South American parasite, destroys the nerves in the bowel wall over years, producing a grossly dilated, paralysed sigmoid colon called a megacolon. This megacolon is dramatically susceptible to twisting, and sigmoid volvulus is the most common bowel emergency in Chagas-endemic regions of South America. Treating Chagas disease early limits the colon damage.

faqs.question: Can constipation cause volvulus?
faqs.answer: Chronic severe constipation does not directly cause volvulus but is a major contributing factor by loading and distending the sigmoid colon over years. In elderly, bed-bound, or opioid-treated patients, a chronically loaded sigmoid colon is more likely to twist. Treating constipation with laxatives, hydration, and mobility is an important preventive measure.

faqs.question: What is the difference between volvulus and ileus?
faqs.answer: Volvulus is a mechanical obstruction from a twisted segment of bowel — there is a physical barrier stopping flow. Paralytic ileus is a functional obstruction where the bowel simply stops contracting; there is no twist or physical block. X-rays and CT distinguish them: volvulus shows a characteristic twisted loop, ileus shows diffuse gas throughout a dilated but untwisted bowel.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after volvulus surgery?
faqs.answer: After laparoscopic elective sigmoid resection, most patients go home in three to four days and recover fully in two to four weeks. After emergency Hartmann's surgery with bowel resection, the hospital stay is seven to fourteen days. If a colostomy was formed, stoma reversal is planned six months later. Newborns after Ladd's procedure typically stay one to three weeks depending on how much bowel was removed.

faqs.question: Can volvulus be prevented?
faqs.answer: Preventing constipation with regular laxatives, adequate fluids, and daily movement significantly reduces sigmoid volvulus risk in elderly patients. After a first successfully decompressed episode, elective sigmoid resection prevents the majority of recurrences. In infants with diagnosed intestinal malrotation, elective Ladd's procedure before a volvulus occurs is strongly considered to avoid the catastrophic midgut complication.

faqs.question: What is the mortality rate for volvulus?
faqs.answer: In uncomplicated sigmoid volvulus treated promptly, mortality is below 3-5% in high-income centres. Once bowel necrosis or perforation occurs, emergency surgery carries a mortality of 15-25%, reflecting the typical elderly, frail patient population. Neonatal midgut volvulus treated before necrosis has near 100% survival; after extensive necrosis, survival depends on how much bowel can be preserved.

faqs.question: What is malrotation and how is it related to volvulus?
faqs.answer: Intestinal malrotation is when the gut does not complete its normal 270-degree rotation during fetal development, leaving the small bowel on a narrow, unstable mesentery. This means the entire small bowel can twist (midgut volvulus) at any point in life, not just in newborns. Most cases present in the first year but malrotation can remain undetected into adulthood and first present as an episode of volvulus.

metaDescription: Volvulus is a dangerous bowel twist causing obstruction and ischaemia. Learn symptoms, emergency diagnosis, enema vs surgery treatment options. Doctor-reviewed.
